examples.title: Breadth-First Search (BFS)
examples.description: BFS algorithm using queue to explore graph level by level
examples.code:
ALGORITHM BFS(G, start)
BEGIN
  Create empty Queue Q
  Create visited array of size |V|, mark all as false
  
  Q.enqueue(start)
  visited[start] = true
  
  WHILE Q is not empty DO
    vertex = Q.dequeue()
    PRINT vertex
    
    FOR each neighbor v of vertex DO
      IF visited[v] = false THEN
        visited[v] = true
        Q.enqueue(v)
      END IF
    END FOR
  END WHILE
END
examples.explanation: BFS starts from source vertex, marks it visited, enqueues it. Then iteratively dequeues a vertex, prints it, and enqueues all unvisited neighbors. This ensures vertices are visited in order of their distance from source.

examples.title: Depth-First Search (DFS) - Recursive
examples.description: Recursive DFS using system call stack
examples.code:
ALGORITHM DFS_Recursive(G, vertex, visited)
BEGIN
  visited[vertex] = true
  PRINT vertex
  
  FOR each neighbor v of vertex DO
    IF visited[v] = false THEN
      DFS_Recursive(G, v, visited)
    END IF
  END FOR
END

ALGORITHM DFS(G)
BEGIN
  Create visited array of size |V|, mark all as false
  
  FOR each vertex v in G DO
    IF visited[v] = false THEN
      DFS_Recursive(G, v, visited)
    END IF
  END FOR
END
examples.explanation: DFS marks current vertex as visited, prints it, then recursively explores each unvisited neighbor. This goes deep along each branch before backtracking. The outer loop ensures all components are visited.

examples.title: Cycle Detection in Undirected Graph using DFS
examples.description: Detects cycle by checking for back edges during DFS
examples.code:
ALGORITHM hasCycle(G)
BEGIN
  Create visited array of size |V|, mark all as false
  
  FOR each vertex v in G DO
    IF visited[v] = false THEN
      IF DFS_Cycle(G, v, -1, visited) = true THEN
        RETURN true
      END IF
    END IF
  END FOR
  RETURN false
END

ALGORITHM DFS_Cycle(G, vertex, parent, visited)
BEGIN
  visited[vertex] = true
  
  FOR each neighbor v of vertex DO
    IF visited[v] = false THEN
      IF DFS_Cycle(G, v, vertex, visited) = true THEN
        RETURN true
      END IF
    ELSE IF v ≠ parent THEN
      // Found visited vertex that is not parent - cycle!
      RETURN true
    END IF
  END FOR
  RETURN false
END
examples.explanation: During DFS, when we visit a neighbor that is already visited and is NOT the parent of current vertex, we've found a back edge which forms a cycle. The parent check prevents false positives from the edge we just came from.

examples.title: Topological Sort using DFS
examples.description: Generates topological ordering of vertices in a DAG
examples.code:
ALGORITHM TopologicalSort(G)
BEGIN
  Create visited array of size |V|, mark all as false
  Create empty Stack S
  
  FOR each vertex v in G DO
    IF visited[v] = false THEN
      DFS_Topo(G, v, visited, S)
    END IF
  END FOR
  
  // Pop from stack to get topological order
  WHILE S is not empty DO
    PRINT S.pop()
  END WHILE
END

ALGORITHM DFS_Topo(G, vertex, visited, S)
BEGIN
  visited[vertex] = true
  
  FOR each neighbor v of vertex DO
    IF visited[v] = false THEN
      DFS_Topo(G, v, visited, S)
    END IF
  END FOR
  
  // Push to stack after all neighbors are processed
  S.push(vertex)
END
examples.explanation: In DFS, a vertex is pushed onto the stack only after all its descendants have been processed. When we pop all vertices from the stack, we get vertices in reverse topological order. This works only on DAGs - cycles would prevent completion.
